title: The Green-Tape Ledgers
theme: Truth is not a parlor trick: a locked room opens only when the detective is willing to expose the respectable greed around it.
style: Victorian London gaslight detective fiction, told in a restrained late-nineteenth-century register: precise, observant, morally shadowed, with Whitechapel grit, drawing-room unease, and every domestic object made to seem capable of testimony.
goal_statement: Solve Malachi Harrow’s locked-room murder at Number 17 Bleakstone Square: name who killed him and explain how the sealed study was made to lie.
intro: In the gaslit winter of 1888, Number 17 Bleakstone Square stands behind its polished knocker and drawn curtains, a respectable London house with Whitechapel mud not far from its doorstep. Malachi Harrow has been found dead in his sealed study among green-taped ledgers, while the household below preserves its manners as carefully as its accounts. You are the one called in after the police have admired the locked door too much; here, a blotter, a hearth-rug, or a servant’s pause may speak more plainly than any gentleman at table.
premise: In 1888 London, the story moves between No. 17 Bleakstone Square and Whitechapel, where Dr. Ada Merrow runs a dispensary and Beatrice Harrow lives under her uncle’s roof after her father’s death in a railway investment. The central machinery is the respectable paper world of green-taped ledgers, inheritances, investments, allowances, and charitable accounts, which turns money into authority and leaves dependents, patients, and households vulnerable to those who control the books.
